quiz_questions.question_text: According to the sliding filament theory, muscle contraction occurs when:

options.text: The thick and thin filaments both shorten in length
Thin (actin) filaments slide past thick (myosin) filaments, shortening the sarcomere
New sarcomeres are created within the muscle fiber
Myosin filaments dissolve and reform in a shorter configuration

quiz_questions.explanation: The sliding filament theory states that muscle contraction results from actin (thin) filaments sliding inward over stationary myosin (thick) filaments, pulling the Z lines closer together and shortening the sarcomere without any filament changing its own length.

quiz_questions.question_text: Thick filaments in the sarcomere are composed of the protein:

options.text: Actin
Troponin
Myosin
Tropomyosin

quiz_questions.explanation: Thick filaments are composed of hundreds of myosin molecules, each with a globular head that contains an actin-binding site and an ATPase enzyme, making myosin the molecular motor of muscle contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Thin filaments are primarily composed of:

options.text: Myosin with associated troponin
Actin along with the regulatory proteins troponin and tropomyosin
Collagen and elastin fibers
Titin and nebulin only

quiz_questions.explanation: Thin filaments consist of two twisted strands of globular actin (G-actin) forming filamentous actin (F-actin), with tropomyosin strands lying in the grooves and troponin complexes spaced at regular intervals along the tropomyosin.

quiz_questions.question_text: Contraction begins when calcium ions (Ca2+) bind to:

options.text: Myosin heads
Actin directly
Troponin
Tropomyosin

quiz_questions.explanation: Calcium ions released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum bind specifically to troponin C (the calcium-binding subunit of the troponin complex), causing a conformational change that shifts tropomyosin away from the actin binding sites.

quiz_questions.question_text: When calcium binds to troponin, what happens next?

options.text: Actin filaments shorten in length
Tropomyosin shifts position, exposing the active binding sites on actin
Myosin dissolves and releases energy
ATP is immediately produced by the sarcomere

quiz_questions.explanation: The calcium-troponin interaction causes troponin to change shape, which physically moves the tropomyosin strand deeper into the groove of the actin helix, uncovering the myosin-binding sites on actin and allowing cross-bridge formation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cross-bridge forms when:

options.text: ATP binds to the myosin head and detaches it from actin
The myosin head binds to the exposed active site on the actin filament
Calcium is pumped back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum
Tropomyosin covers the actin binding sites

quiz_questions.explanation: Once tropomyosin shifts and actin binding sites are exposed, the energized (cocked) myosin head attaches to the actin filament, forming the cross-bridge that is essential for generating the power stroke.

quiz_questions.question_text: The power stroke is energized by:

options.text: A new ATP molecule binding during the power stroke
Calcium ions directly pushing the myosin head
Energy stored in the myosin head from the prior hydrolysis of ATP
Sodium ions entering the muscle fiber

quiz_questions.explanation: Before the power stroke, ATP was hydrolyzed to ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi), and this energy was stored as potential energy in the cocked myosin head; when the cross-bridge forms, this stored energy is released to pivot the head and pull actin inward.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the power stroke, which molecules are released from the myosin head?

options.text: A new ATP molecule
Calcium ions (Ca2+)
ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi)
Troponin and tropomyosin

quiz_questions.explanation: As the myosin head pivots during the power stroke, it releases ADP and Pi (the products of the previous ATP hydrolysis), completing the energy transfer that pulls the actin filament toward the center of the sarcomere.

quiz_questions.question_text: Detachment of the myosin head from actin requires:

options.text: More calcium ions
ADP binding to the myosin head
A new ATP molecule binding to the myosin head
No additional molecules; detachment is spontaneous

quiz_questions.explanation: After the power stroke, the myosin head remains bound to actin in a rigor state until a fresh ATP molecule binds to the myosin head, causing a conformational change that releases the cross-bridge and allows the cycle to continue.

quiz_questions.question_text: Rigor mortis occurs after death because:

options.text: Muscles relax completely without nerve stimulation
No ATP is available to detach myosin from actin, leaving muscles stiff
Excess calcium causes muscles to grow after death
Muscles contract faster after death due to lack of inhibition

quiz_questions.explanation: After death, ATP production ceases and calcium leaks out of the SR; without ATP to break the actin-myosin cross-bridges, the myosin heads remain permanently attached to actin, causing the characteristic stiffness of rigor mortis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a cross-bridge in muscle contraction?

options.text: A connection between two adjacent muscle fibers
The attachment formed when a myosin head binds to an active site on actin during contraction
A bridge of connective tissue between two fascicles
The gap junction between two cardiac cells

quiz_questions.explanation: A cross-bridge refers to the temporary bond formed when the globular head of a myosin molecule attaches to a binding site on an actin filament, creating the structural link through which force is transmitted to pull actin inward during the power stroke.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the power stroke?

options.text: The step where ATP binds to myosin and detaches it from actin
The pivoting motion of the myosin head that pulls the actin filament toward the M line, generating force
The release of calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
The nerve impulse traveling along the sarcolemma

quiz_questions.explanation: The power stroke is the force-generating step of the cross-bridge cycle in which the myosin head pivots from its cocked position to a bent position, pulling the attached actin filament approximately 10 nanometers toward the center of the sarcomere.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of tropomyosin in muscle contraction?

options.text: It provides the energy for the power stroke
It is a regulatory protein that blocks myosin-binding sites on actin when the muscle is at rest
It stores calcium ions for release during contraction
It forms the thick filament core

quiz_questions.explanation: Tropomyosin is a long, rod-shaped regulatory protein that winds along the actin filament groove, physically blocking the myosin-binding sites when the muscle is relaxed and shifting position when calcium binds to troponin to permit cross-bridge formation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of troponin in the contraction cycle?

options.text: It hydrolyzes ATP to provide energy for the myosin head
It is a regulatory protein complex on the thin filament that binds calcium and causes tropomyosin to shift, exposing actin binding sites
It forms the core structure of the thick filament
It pumps calcium ions back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum

quiz_questions.explanation: Troponin is a three-subunit regulatory complex (troponin C binds calcium, troponin T binds tropomyosin, troponin I inhibits actin-myosin interaction) that acts as the calcium sensor on the thin filament, initiating contraction when calcium binds to troponin C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the cross-bridge cycle mean?

options.text: A single contraction and relaxation event of the whole muscle
The repeating sequence of myosin head attachment, pivoting (power stroke), detachment, and re-cocking that produces muscle contraction
The transmission of an action potential from one muscle fiber to the next
The process of building new sarcomeres during muscle growth

quiz_questions.explanation: The cross-bridge cycle is the repetitive four-step process of attachment (cross-bridge formation), power stroke (pivoting), detachment (ATP binding), and re-cocking (ATP hydrolysis) that occurs many times during a single contraction to progressively slide actin along myosin.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'cocking' of the myosin head mean?

options.text: The myosin head permanently detaches from actin and breaks down
The myosin head hydrolyzes ATP to ADP and Pi, storing the energy as the head returns to its high-energy (upright) position
Calcium binds directly to the myosin head, activating it
The actin filament wraps around the myosin head to lock it in place

quiz_questions.explanation: Cocking refers to the process where the myosin head, after binding and hydrolyzing ATP to ADP + Pi, uses the released energy to return to its high-energy (upright or extended) position, ready to bind actin and perform another power stroke.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is excitation-contraction coupling?

options.text: The direct transmission of force from one muscle to another
The sequence of events linking the electrical excitation of the sarcolemma to the mechanical contraction of myofibrils via calcium release from the SR
The connection between the brain and the muscle via motor neurons
The process of repairing damaged muscle fibers after exercise

quiz_questions.explanation: Excitation-contraction coupling is the physiological process that converts an electrical signal (action potential on the sarcolemma and T-tubules) into a mechanical response (muscle contraction) through the release of calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'rigor state' mean in the context of the cross-bridge cycle?

options.text: The state when the muscle is fully relaxed and no cross-bridges exist
The state where the myosin head is firmly bound to actin after the power stroke but before new ATP binds to release it
The state when calcium floods the sarcoplasm causing permanent contraction
The state of maximum sarcomere elongation

quiz_questions.explanation: The rigor state occurs after the power stroke when the myosin head is tightly bound to actin in a low-energy configuration; it persists until a new ATP molecule binds to the myosin head to break the bond, which is why ATP depletion leads to rigor mortis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Ca2+-ATPase pump?

options.text: A channel that allows calcium to flow passively into the sarcoplasm
An active transport protein in the SR membrane that uses ATP energy to pump calcium ions back into the SR, causing muscle relaxation
An enzyme that breaks down myosin during relaxation
A receptor on the motor end plate that binds acetylcholine

quiz_questions.explanation: The Ca2+-ATPase (SERCA) pump is an active transport protein embedded in the sarcoplasmic reticulum membrane that uses ATP to transport calcium ions from the sarcoplasm back into the SR lumen, lowering cytoplasmic calcium and allowing tropomyosin to re-cover actin binding sites for relaxation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'relaxation' mean at the molecular level in a muscle fiber?

options.text: Myosin heads permanently detach and are degraded
Calcium is actively pumped back into the SR, troponin releases calcium, tropomyosin re-covers actin binding sites, and cross-bridges can no longer form
The sarcolemma depolarizes and generates a new action potential
New sarcomeres are added to lengthen the muscle fiber

quiz_questions.explanation: Molecular relaxation occurs when Ca2+-ATPase pumps remove calcium from the sarcoplasm, causing calcium to dissociate from troponin, allowing tropomyosin to return to its blocking position over the actin binding sites, preventing further cross-bridge formation and letting the muscle passively return to its resting length.

quiz_questions.question_text: A forensic scientist examining a body found in a warm apartment notices the muscles are very stiff. The estimated time of death is 8 hours prior. Using knowledge of the sliding filament theory, which molecular event best explains the stiffness?

options.text: Calcium has been completely removed from all muscle fibers
ATP depletion after death prevents myosin heads from detaching from actin, locking the cross-bridges in place
The muscles are contracting voluntarily due to residual brain activity
Excess ATP production after death causes super-contraction of all muscles

quiz_questions.explanation: After death, cellular metabolism stops and ATP is rapidly depleted; without ATP to bind myosin and break the actin-myosin cross-bridges, the myosin heads remain locked to actin, causing the characteristic stiffness of rigor mortis that typically begins 2-6 hours after death.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is studying a patient with malignant hyperthermia, a condition where the sarcoplasmic reticulum releases massive amounts of calcium uncontrollably during anesthesia. Based on the sliding filament theory, what would happen to the patient's muscles?

options.text: The muscles would become completely paralyzed and flaccid
The muscles would undergo sustained, uncontrolled contraction and generate dangerous amounts of heat
The muscles would function normally despite the excess calcium
Only smooth muscle would be affected

quiz_questions.explanation: Massive uncontrolled calcium release would flood the sarcoplasm, binding all available troponin, exposing all actin binding sites, and causing sustained, widespread cross-bridge cycling that produces sustained contraction, enormous ATP consumption, and dangerous heat generation (hyperthermia).

quiz_questions.question_text: A physiology professor demonstrates that when a muscle fiber is placed in a solution with no ATP, the muscle becomes rigid and stiff. A student then adds ATP to the solution and observes the muscle relax. What step of the cross-bridge cycle does this experiment demonstrate?

options.text: The power stroke step, where myosin pivots and pulls actin
The detachment step, where binding of new ATP to the myosin head breaks the cross-bridge
The cocking step, where the myosin head returns to its high-energy position
The calcium-binding step, where calcium attaches to troponin

quiz_questions.explanation: This experiment demonstrates that ATP is required for cross-bridge detachment; without ATP, myosin remains bound to actin (rigor), and when ATP is added, it binds to the myosin head, causing a conformational change that releases the cross-bridge and allows relaxation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient is diagnosed with a rare genetic condition in which their troponin molecules cannot bind calcium. Based on the sliding filament theory, how would this affect muscle contraction?

options.text: Muscles would be locked in permanent contraction
Muscles would be unable to contract because tropomyosin would permanently block the actin binding sites
Muscles would contract normally using an alternative pathway
Only cardiac muscle would be affected, not skeletal muscle

quiz_questions.explanation: If troponin cannot bind calcium, the calcium-troponin-tropomyosin regulatory mechanism fails; tropomyosin remains in its blocking position over the actin binding sites even when calcium is present, preventing myosin from forming cross-bridges and making contraction impossible.

quiz_questions.question_text: An athlete notices that after an intense sprint, her legs feel heavy and weak. Her coach explains that during intense exercise, calcium pumps in the SR may not keep up with demand. How does impaired calcium reuptake relate to the sliding filament theory?

options.text: Impaired calcium reuptake would cause muscles to relax faster than normal
Impaired calcium reuptake means calcium stays in the sarcoplasm longer, which can lead to prolonged cross-bridge cycling, cramping, and eventual fatigue
Calcium reuptake has no effect on the cross-bridge cycle
Impaired calcium reuptake would increase ATP production

quiz_questions.explanation: When Ca2+-ATPase pumps cannot efficiently return calcium to the SR, elevated sarcoplasmic calcium keeps troponin activated and actin binding sites exposed, sustaining cross-bridge cycling and preventing proper relaxation, which contributes to cramping and accelerates fatigue through excessive ATP consumption.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmacology student is learning about dantrolene, a drug used to treat malignant hyperthermia. The drug works by blocking calcium release channels on the sarcoplasmic reticulum. Using the sliding filament theory, explain why this drug stops the dangerous muscle contractions.

options.text: It increases ATP production so myosin can detach faster
It blocks calcium release from the SR, so troponin remains inactive, tropomyosin stays blocking actin, and cross-bridges cannot form
It destroys the myosin heads so they cannot attach to actin
It stimulates acetylcholinesterase to break down ACh faster

quiz_questions.explanation: By blocking the ryanodine receptors (calcium release channels) on the SR, dantrolene prevents calcium from entering the sarcoplasm, keeping troponin in its calcium-free state and tropomyosin in its blocking position, which halts cross-bridge formation and stops the dangerous sustained contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a physiology exam, a student is asked: 'If a myosin head could not hydrolyze ATP, what would happen to the cross-bridge cycle?' What is the correct answer?

options.text: The cycle would proceed normally because ATP hydrolysis is not required
The myosin head could not be re-cocked to its high-energy position, so after detachment it could not perform another power stroke
The power stroke would be twice as strong to compensate
Calcium would substitute for ATP in the cocking step

quiz_questions.explanation: ATP hydrolysis by the myosin ATPase is essential for re-energizing the myosin head; if hydrolysis cannot occur, the myosin head cannot return to its cocked (high-energy) position after detachment, meaning it cannot perform another power stroke and the cross-bridge cycle would stall.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nursing student is reviewing a case where a patient was exposed to a toxin that prevents ATP from binding to myosin heads. The patient's muscles became extremely rigid. Using the cross-bridge cycle, explain why the muscles became rigid instead of relaxed.

options.text: Without ATP binding, more calcium would be released from the SR
Without ATP binding to myosin, the cross-bridges cannot be broken after the power stroke, leaving myosin permanently attached to actin
The toxin would cause the muscles to relax, not become rigid
ATP is only needed for contraction, not for detachment

quiz_questions.explanation: ATP binding to myosin is required to break the actin-myosin cross-bridge after the power stroke; if ATP cannot bind, the myosin heads remain locked to actin in a rigor-like state, causing sustained rigidity similar to rigor mortis even in a living patient.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher is developing a new muscle relaxant drug. She wants the drug to prevent muscle contraction without affecting nerve signaling. Based on the sliding filament theory, which target would be most specific for stopping contraction at the sarcomere level?

options.text: Block acetylcholine receptors at the NMJ
Block the calcium release channels on the sarcoplasmic reticulum so calcium cannot reach troponin
Destroy all motor neurons leading to the muscle
Remove all connective tissue from around the muscle

quiz_questions.explanation: Blocking calcium release channels on the SR would prevent calcium from reaching the sarcoplasm and binding troponin, keeping tropomyosin in its blocking position and preventing cross-bridge formation, all without interfering with neural transmission at the NMJ.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student athlete asks her professor why muscles need both ATP and calcium to contract and why running out of either one would be a problem. Which response best applies the sliding filament theory?

options.text: Calcium and ATP serve the same function and are interchangeable
Calcium is needed to expose actin binding sites (via troponin), and ATP is needed to energize the myosin head, detach cross-bridges, and pump calcium back into the SR; without either, the cycle stops
Only ATP is necessary; calcium plays no direct role in the cross-bridge cycle
Only calcium is necessary; ATP is used exclusively for nerve signaling

quiz_questions.explanation: Calcium is the trigger that permits cross-bridge formation by activating troponin and moving tropomyosin, while ATP provides energy for cocking the myosin head, detaching cross-bridges after the power stroke, and powering the SR calcium pumps for relaxation; both are indispensable for the complete contraction-relaxation cycle.
